original_abstract:
Multimodal visual information fusion aims to integrate multi-sensor data into a single composite image that maximizes complementary information while minimizing feature redundancy. However, extracting such information is challenging, particularly for infrared and visible images, due to the substantial modality gap. While standard cross-attention modules typically prioritize feature correlation, effective image fusion requires a focus on complementarity (uncorrelation). 

In this paper, we propose CrossFuse, a novel fusion approach utilizing a specialized Cross-Attention Mechanism (CAM) designed specifically to enhance complementary information. We employ a two-stage training strategy: first, we train two identical auto-encoder architectures to reconstruct their respective source modalities; second, we freeze the encoders and train the CAM alongside a decoder to perform the fusion. Within this framework, the CAM integrates features from both modalities into a unified representation where complementary details are boosted and redundant features are suppressed. Finally, the trained decoder reconstructs the fused image from these optimized features. Experimental results demonstrate that CrossFuse achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple benchmarks compared to existing fusion networks. The source code for our method will be made available.
humanized_abstract:
Multimodal visual information fusion refers to a process by which the useful complementary information from multi-sensor data is synthesized into a fused image that minimizes redundant features. Due to large modality gap, extracting useful information from infrared and visible images is a very challenging task. Though the cross-attention module in deep learning has considered the feature correlation during the attention process, image fusion based on deep learning model needs to consider uncorrelation rather than the correlation between features.

In this paper, we propose CrossFuse, a fusion approach that leverages a Cross-Attention Mechanism (CAM) to explore the complementary information between different modalities. We train two identical auto-encoder networks in two stages: 1) to learn to reconstruct the input modality; 2) to learn a Cross-Attention Mechanism (CAM) and a decoder network, while keeping the encoder network frozen to fuse the complementary information between different modalities. The CAM learns to aggregate the features from different modalities to obtain a fused feature map where the complementary information is highlighted and the redundant information is suppressed, which is then reconstructed into the fused image by the decoder. Experimental results show that CrossFuse achieves the best performance on a variety of benchmarks compared with the state-of-the-art fusion networks. Our code will be available.

Experimental results show that CrossFuse achieves the best performance on a variety of benchmarks compared with the state-of-the-art fusion networks. Our code will be available.
